question: What is the difference between an array and a linked list? When would you choose one over the other?
idealAnswer: An array stores elements in contiguous memory locations, allowing O(1) random access via index but O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle. A linked list stores elements in nodes with pointers, allowing O(1) insertion/deletion at head but O(n) access. Choose arrays when you need fast random access and know the size upfront. Choose linked lists when you need frequent insertions/deletions and the size is dynamic.

question: Write a C function to reverse a singly linked list iteratively.
hints: Use three pointers: prev, current, next

question: What is a stack? Explain its operations and give two real-world applications of stacks.
idealAnswer: A stack is a linear data structure that follows LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle. Main operations: push (add to top, O(1)), pop (remove from top, O(1)), peek/top (view top element, O(1)), isEmpty. Real-world applications: (1) Function call stack — the runtime uses a stack to track function calls and return addresses. (2) Undo/Redo operations in text editors. Other examples: expression evaluation, bracket matching, browser back button.

question: Compare Stack and Queue data structures with at least 4 differences.
idealAnswer: 1) Ordering: Stack uses LIFO (Last In First Out), Queue uses FIFO (First In First Out). 2) Operations: Stack has push/pop from same end (top), Queue has enqueue at rear and dequeue from front. 3) Access point: Stack has one open end (top), Queue has two open ends (front and rear). 4) Use cases: Stack is used for recursion/backtracking/undo, Queue is used for scheduling/BFS/buffering. 5) Example: Stack = stack of plates, Queue = line at a ticket counter.

question: Explain the three types of binary tree traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder) with an example.
hints: Think of the root's position: in-between, pre, post
idealAnswer: For a tree with root=1, left=2, right=3: Inorder (Left, Root, Right): visits left subtree, then root, then right subtree. Result: 2, 1, 3. Used to get sorted order in BST. Preorder (Root, Left, Right): visits root first, then left, then right subtree. Result: 1, 2, 3. Used to create a copy of the tree. Postorder (Left, Right, Root): visits left, then right, then root last. Result: 2, 3, 1. Used to delete the tree.

question: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST)? What are its time complexities for search, insert, and delete in the best and worst cases?
idealAnswer: A BST is a binary tree where for each node: all values in the left subtree are less than the node, and all values in the right subtree are greater. This property enables efficient searching. Best/Average case (balanced tree): Search O(log n), Insert O(log n), Delete O(log n). Worst case (skewed tree — all nodes on one side): Search O(n), Insert O(n), Delete O(n). The worst case occurs when elements are inserted in sorted order, making the tree degenerate into a linked list.

question: What is a hash table? Explain how collisions are handled using chaining and open addressing.
idealAnswer: A hash table is a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function. The hash function converts a key into an index in an array. Average case operations are O(1). Collision occurs when two keys hash to the same index. Chaining: Each slot has a linked list; colliding elements are added to the list at that index. Simple but uses extra memory. Open Addressing: All elements stored in the array itself. On collision, probe for the next empty slot using: Linear Probing (check next slot), Quadratic Probing (check i² slots away), or Double Hashing (use second hash function).
keyPoints: hash function maps key to index
open addressing probes for empty slots

question: Compare the time and space complexities of Bubble Sort, Merge Sort, and Quick Sort. Which one would you choose for a large dataset and why?
idealAnswer: Bubble Sort: Time O(n²) average/worst, O(n) best (already sorted), Space O(1). Simple but slow for large data. Merge Sort: Time O(n log n) all cases, Space O(n) — needs extra array. Stable sort, consistent performance. Quick Sort: Time O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst (bad pivot), Space O(log n) for recursion stack. In-place. For large datasets, Quick Sort is usually preferred because it has good cache performance and O(log n) space. Merge Sort is preferred when stability is needed or when worst-case O(n log n) guarantee is required.
keyPoints: Quick O(n log n) avg in-place
Quick Sort preferred for large data

question: Explain the difference between BFS and DFS graph traversals. When would you use each?
idealAnswer: BFS (Breadth-First Search): Uses a queue. Explores all neighbors at current depth before moving deeper. Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs. Time O(V+E), Space O(V). Use for: shortest path, level-order traversal, finding closest nodes. DFS (Depth-First Search): Uses a stack (or recursion). Explores as deep as possible along a branch before backtracking. Time O(V+E), Space O(V). Use for: cycle detection, topological sort, finding connected components, maze solving. BFS is better for shortest paths, DFS is better for exploring all possibilities.
keyPoints: BFS uses queue, DFS uses stack

question: What is Big O notation? Explain with examples of O(1), O(n), O(log n), and O(n²).
idealAnswer: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time complexity — how the running time grows as input size grows. O(1) — Constant: Accessing array element by index. Time doesn't change with input size. O(log n) — Logarithmic: Binary search. Halves the search space each step. O(n) — Linear: Linear search, traversing an array. Time grows proportionally with input. O(n²) — Quadratic: Nested loops like Bubble Sort. Time grows as square of input. Order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2^n).

question: Write a C/C++ function to check if a string of parentheses is balanced. For example, '(())' is balanced, '(()' is not.
hints: Push opening brackets, pop on closing brackets

question: What is a heap data structure? Explain the difference between min-heap and max-heap.
idealAnswer: A heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property. It is typically implemented using an array. Min-Heap: The parent node is always smaller than or equal to its children. The root contains the minimum element. Used in: priority queues (minimum priority first), Dijkstra's algorithm. Max-Heap: The parent node is always greater than or equal to its children. The root contains the maximum element. Used in: heap sort, maximum priority queue. Key operations: Insert O(log n) — add at end, bubble up. Delete min/max O(log n) — remove root, replace with last element, bubble down. Build heap O(n).

question: Write a C function to perform binary search on a sorted array. Return the index of the target element or -1 if not found.
What are the two common ways to represent a graph in memory? Compare them.
hints: Think about adjacency
idealAnswer: 1) Adjacency Matrix: A 2D array of size V×V where matrix[i][j] = 1 if there's an edge between vertex i and j. Space: O(V²). Edge lookup: O(1). Good for: dense graphs, when you need fast edge existence checks. 2) Adjacency List: An array of lists where each index stores a list of its neighbors. Space: O(V+E). Edge lookup: O(degree). Good for: sparse graphs (most real-world graphs), when you need to iterate over neighbors. Most algorithms use adjacency list because real graphs are usually sparse.

question: What is Dynamic Programming? How is it different from recursion? Explain with the Fibonacci example.
hints: Think about overlapping subproblems
idealAnswer: Dynamic Programming (DP) solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing their results to avoid recomputation. Two key properties: 1) Optimal Substructure — optimal solution can be built from optimal sub-solutions. 2) Overlapping Subproblems — same subproblems are solved multiple times. Fibonacci with plain recursion: fib(5) recomputes fib(3) multiple times. Time: O(2^n). With DP (memoization/tabulation): Store each fib(n) once. Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Memoization = top-down (recursion + cache). Tabulation = bottom-up (iterative, fill table).

question: Trace the steps of Selection Sort on the array [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]. Show the array after each pass.
hints: Find minimum in unsorted part, swap with first unsorted element
idealAnswer:
Selection Sort finds the minimum element in the unsorted part and swaps it with the first unsorted position.
Pass 1: min=11 at idx 4, swap with idx 0 → [11, 25, 12, 22, 64]
Pass 2: min=12 at idx 2, swap with idx 1 → [11, 12, 25, 22, 64]
Pass 3: min=22 at idx 3, swap with idx 2 → [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]
Pass 4: min=25 at idx 3, already in place → [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]
Final: [11, 12, 22, 25, 64]. Time: O(n²) all cases. Not stable.

question: What is a circular queue? Why is it better than a simple linear queue?
idealAnswer: A circular queue connects the last position back to the first position, forming a circle. In a linear queue using an array, after dequeue operations, front moves forward and the space at the beginning is wasted even if empty — this is the problem of 'false overflow'. Circular queue solves this by using modular arithmetic: rear = (rear + 1) % size, front = (front + 1) % size. This reuses the wasted space. It is full when (rear + 1) % size == front. It is empty when front == rear. This makes the queue memory-efficient without needing to shift elements.

question: Write a C function to find the middle element of a singly linked list in one pass.
hints: Use two pointers moving at different speeds

question: What is the difference between a binary tree and a binary search tree (BST)? Can you convert a BST to a sorted array?
idealAnswer: A binary tree is a tree where each node has at most 2 children. There is no ordering constraint. A BST is a binary tree with an ordering property: left child < root < right child for every node. To convert a BST to a sorted array, perform an inorder traversal (left, root, right). Since BST's inorder traversal always produces elements in ascending sorted order, you simply collect the elements during traversal. Time: O(n), Space: O(n) for the result array.

question: Write a C/C++ program to find the first non-repeating character in a string using a hash map (frequency array).
hints: Use an array of size 256 for ASCII characters
Count the total number of iterations
idealAnswer: The outer loop runs n times. The inner loop runs (n-i) times for each i. Total iterations: n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²). Even though the inner loop doesn't always run n times, the sum of the series is n(n+1)/2 which is still quadratic. So the time complexity is O(n²).

question: How would you implement a queue using two stacks? Explain the approach and its time complexity.
hints: One stack for enqueue, one for dequeue
idealAnswer: Use two stacks: stack1 (for enqueue) and stack2 (for dequeue). Enqueue: Push element onto stack1. O(1). Dequeue: If stack2 is empty, pop all elements from stack1 and push onto stack2 (this reverses the order). Then pop from stack2. If stack2 is not empty, just pop from stack2. Amortized time for dequeue is O(1) because each element is moved at most once. Worst case for a single dequeue is O(n) but amortized over n operations it's O(1) per operation.

question: Write a C function to find the second largest element in an array of integers.
hints: Can you do it in a single pass?

question: What is a topological sort? When can it be applied? Give an example use case.
hints: Think about dependencies
idealAnswer: Topological sort is a linear ordering of vertices in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) such that for every directed edge (u, v), u comes before v in the ordering. It can ONLY be applied to DAGs — graphs with directed edges and no cycles. If the graph has a cycle, topological sort is not possible. Can be done using DFS (reverse post-order) or BFS (Kahn's algorithm using in-degree). Use cases: Task scheduling (prerequisite courses), build systems (compile order), package dependency resolution. Example: If course A is prerequisite for B, and B for C, topological order is A, B, C.

question: Write a C function to move all zeros in an array to the end while maintaining the relative order of non-zero elements. For example, [0, 1, 0, 3, 12] becomes [1, 3, 12, 0, 0].
hints: Use a write pointer to track where non-zero elements should go

question: What is a balanced binary tree? Name two self-balancing BST types and explain why balancing matters.
idealAnswer: A balanced binary tree is one where the height difference between left and right subtrees of every node is at most 1. Two self-balancing BSTs: 1) AVL Tree — strictly balanced (height difference ≤ 1). Uses rotations (left, right, left-right, right-left) to rebalance after insert/delete. Faster lookup but slower insert/delete due to frequent rotations. 2) Red-Black Tree — loosely balanced using color properties. Less rotations than AVL but slightly less balanced. Used in C++ STL map/set, Java TreeMap. Balancing matters because an unbalanced BST degenerates to O(n) operations, while balanced BSTs guarantee O(log n).

question: What does it mean for a sorting algorithm to be 'stable'? Give one example of a stable and one unstable sorting algorithm.
idealAnswer: A stable sorting algorithm preserves the relative order of elements with equal keys. If two elements have equal values, they appear in the same order in the sorted output as in the input. Stable: Merge Sort, Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort. Unstable: Quick Sort, Heap Sort, Selection Sort. Stability matters when sorting by multiple criteria. For example, if you sort students by name (already sorted by roll number), a stable sort keeps same-name students in roll number order.
keyPoints: preserves relative order of equal elements

question: Write a C function to find the nth Fibonacci number using dynamic programming (bottom-up tabulation).
You need to implement a recent browsing history feature. Which data structure would you use and why? How would you handle a maximum size limit?
hints: Think about which end you add to and remove from
idealAnswer: Use a doubly linked list combined with a hash map (similar to LRU Cache). When a new page is visited: add it to the front of the list. If it already exists, move it to the front. When the size exceeds the limit, remove the node at the tail (oldest entry). The hash map provides O(1) lookup to check if a page is already in the history. Alternatively, a simpler approach: use a stack (LIFO) for back navigation with a fixed-size array/circular buffer. The doubly linked list + hash map gives O(1) for all operations: add, remove, lookup, move-to-front.
keyPoints: doubly linked list for O(1) add/remove
remove oldest from tail when full

question: Sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass without using a sorting library (Dutch National Flag problem). Example: [2,0,1,2,1,0] -> [0,0,1,1,2,2].
hints: Three pointers: low, mid, high
Each element is examined at most once

hints: A min-heap of size k
Or Quickselect for average O(n)

question: Given strings s and t, find the minimum window substring of s that contains all characters of t (including duplicates). Example: s="ADOBECODEBANC", t="ABC" -> "BANC". Return "" if none exists.
